explanation: The natural language statement "prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" means that `prop_0` is not permitted to be true until the combined condition of `prop_1`, `prop_2`, `prop_3`, and `prop_4` has become true. This is a classic precedence property. In LTL, the `U` (Until) operator is used to express this: `!A U B` means 'A must be false until B becomes true'. Applying this pattern, `!prop_0` must hold until `(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)` becomes true.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" implies that for 'prop_0' to be true, the combined condition '(prop_1 AND prop_2 AND prop_3 AND prop_4)' must also be true. This translates to a global implication: if 'prop_0' is true, then all of 'prop_1', 'prop_2', 'prop_3', and 'prop_4' must also be true concurrently. While 'only after' often suggests a temporal precedence where the enabling conditions occurred strictly in the past, in Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) without past operators, the most common and least ambiguous direct translation for such a requirement is to enforce concurrency, meaning the condition must hold whenever 'prop_0' is true.

The natural language statement "the prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" implies a temporal ordering: if `prop_0` is true at any point, then the conjunction of `prop_1`, `prop_2`, `prop_3`, and `prop_4` must have been true up to and including that point. Let P be `prop_0` and Q be `(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)`. The phrase "P only after Q" is commonly translated in Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) as `G (P -> (Q W P))`, where `W` denotes the weak until operator. The weak until operator `A W B` means that `A` holds until `B` holds, or `A` holds globally if `B` never holds. In this context, if `prop_0` becomes true, then `(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)` must have been continuously true up to that point. The problem statement explicitly allows the `V` (releases) operator. The `A releases B` operator (`A V B`) is equivalent to `B W A`. Therefore, `(Q W P)` can be directly written as `P V Q`. So, the formula `G (P -> (P V Q))` where `P` is `prop_0` and `Q` is `(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)` expresses this property. The phrase "This property holds for every DM_i" implies that this is a global property, hence the `G` (globally) operator.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1" means that `prop_0` cannot become true unless `prop_1` has already been true at some point (or is true simultaneously). This is translated by forbidding the scenario where `prop_0` becomes true and, up until that point, `prop_1` was never true. The subformula `(!prop_1 U prop_0)` expresses that `prop_0` is true, and `!prop_1` was true at all preceding states until `prop_0` became true. Thus, `F (prop_0 & (!prop_1 U prop_0))` means "Eventually, `prop_0` is true, and `prop_1` had not been true before or at that point". To enforce "prop_0 only after prop_1", we negate this forbidden scenario.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" means that if prop_0 is true at any point, then the conjunction of prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 must have become true at some point prior to or at the same time as prop_0, and prop_0 must have been false during the period leading up to that conjunction becoming true. This is expressed using the 'until' operator. Let P_all be the conjunction (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4). The formula G (prop_0 -> (!prop_0 U P_all)) states that globally, if prop_0 is true, then it must have been the case that prop_0 was false until P_all became true.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" means that if `prop_0` ever occurs, then the conjunction of `prop_1`, `prop_2`, `prop_3`, and `prop_4` must have occurred at some point in the past or at the same time as `prop_0`. Let `Q_all` denote `(prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4)`. The property essentially forbids `prop_0` from occurring if `Q_all` has not yet occurred. This means we forbid any trace where `prop_0` happens, and from the beginning of the trace up to the occurrence of `prop_0`, `Q_all` has been continuously false. The LTL expression `(!Q_all U prop_0)` describes the scenario where `prop_0` eventually happens, and until it happens, `!Q_all` holds. To express that this scenario is globally forbidden, we negate it and apply the global operator `G`.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" implies a temporal precedence. Specifically, if prop_0 becomes true, then the conjunction of prop_1, prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 must have been true at or before that point. In Linear Temporal Logic (LTL), this "P only after Q" relationship is expressed using the Until operator. The formula G(P -> (!P U Q)) means "Globally, if P is true, then P must have been false until Q became true (at which point P can become true)." This ensures that Q is true at or before P becomes true, and P does not become true before Q. Let Q_combined be (prop_1 & prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4). Then, the property translates to G(prop_0 -> (!prop_0 U Q_combined)).

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1" means that any occurrence of `prop_0` must be preceded by or be simultaneous with an occurrence of `prop_1`. In other words, `prop_0` cannot occur if `prop_1` has not occurred before or at the same time. This is a common past-time property translated into future-time LTL. The chosen LTL formula `G(prop_0 -> (prop_1 | X(!prop_0 U prop_1)))` captures this logic:

*   `G(...)`: The property must hold globally (at all times).
*   `prop_0 -> (...)`: If `prop_0` is true at any given time `t`, then the right-hand side must also be true at `t`.
*   `prop_1`: This part allows `prop_0` to be true if `prop_1` is also true at the same time `t`. This covers the "simultaneous" aspect of "only after".
*   `X(!prop_0 U prop_1)`: If `prop_1` is not true at time `t` (when `prop_0` is true), then this part must hold. It means that, starting from the next state (`X`), `prop_0` must remain false (`!prop_0`) until `prop_1` becomes true (`U prop_1`). This effectively ensures that if `prop_0` occurs without `prop_1` being present, `prop_0` must be immediately 'turned off' (i.e., become false in the next state) unless `prop_1` becomes true. This forces `prop_1` to precede or accompany `prop_0`'s occurrence.

The natural language phrase "prop_0 only after prop_1" means that prop_0 cannot become true unless prop_1 has already been true (at the same time or at some previous point). This can be expressed by stating that it is always true that if prop_0 is to occur, then prop_1 must have occurred before or at the same time. The LTL formula captures this by forbidding the scenario where prop_0 becomes true while prop_1 has not yet occurred. Specifically, if it is true that `!prop_1` holds until `prop_0` holds (i.e., `prop_0` occurs without `prop_1` having occurred before it), then `prop_0` must never happen. This ensures that for `prop_0` to occur, `prop_1` must have already been true.

The statement "prop_0 only after prop_1" means that for `prop_0` to occur, `prop_1` must have occurred at or before that point in time. In other words, `prop_0` cannot occur before `prop_1` has ever occurred. This can be expressed using the Release operator `V`. The LTL formula `G (prop_1 V !prop_0)` states that globally, either `!prop_0` (prop_0 is false) is always true, or `!prop_0` is true until `prop_1` becomes true. If `!prop_0` is always true, then `prop_0` never occurs, satisfying the condition trivially. If `!prop_0` is true until `prop_1` becomes true, it means that `prop_0` can only become true once `prop_1` has already been true. This precisely captures the meaning of "prop_0 only after prop_1".
